abstract: Recent breakthroughs in deep learning-based generative models have rapidly transitioned into the domain of time series analysis, offering remarkable performance gains. However, the efficacy of these deep neural networks remains heavily contingent on the availability of large, consistent datasets—a requirement seldom met in real-world scenarios, where data is often scarce, noisy, or subject to strict constraints. To address these limitations, data augmentation has emerged as a critical strategy, ranging from traditional techniques like noise injection and permutation to the sophisticated synthesis of entirely new data points. This paper provides a systematic review of the current state-of-the-art in time series generation, offering a comprehensive overview of available algorithms and introducing a formal taxonomy of the most significant research. Central to this study is an evaluation of the efficiency of different architectural variants, an analysis of the various metrics used to assess performance, and a discussion of the inherent challenges facing each model. Ultimately, this work aims to synthesize the evolution of the field and highlight the most promising methodologies to serve as a roadmap for future research.
